data.title: Okasan Securities Group (TSE:8609) Margin Improvement Challenges Bearish Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Okasan Securities Group (TSE:8609) has posted Q3 2026 revenue of ¥25,351 million and basic EPS of ¥27.88, with trailing 12 month EPS at ¥69.41 and net income of ¥13,920 million reflecting its recent earnings run rate. The company has seen revenue move from ¥19,971 million and EPS of ¥16.64 in Q2 2025 to ¥25,351 million and EPS of ¥27.88 in Q3 2026, while trailing 12 month revenue sits at ¥85,667 million. With net profit margin on the trailing numbers at 16.2%, the latest quarter keeps the focus squarely on how efficiently Okasan is converting its top line into profit.

See our full analysis for Okasan Securities Group.

With the latest results on the table, the next step is to see how these margin and earnings trends line up with the main narratives investors have been following around Okasan Securities Group.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

TTM margin edges up to 16.2%

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, Okasan is converting ¥85,667 million of revenue into ¥13,920 million of net income, which works out to a 16.2% net profit margin compared with 15.3% a year earlier.
  • What stands out for a cautious, bearish view that focuses on future declines is that this 16.2% margin and 7.9% trailing earnings growth sit alongside modelled forecasts for about 27.1% per year earnings decline, creating a gap between the recent profitability trend and those weaker forward expectations.
    • Bears point to the projected earnings decline and expected revenue decline of about 3.4% per year, while the latest trailing figures still show ¥13,920 million of net income on ¥85,667 million of revenue.
    • This contrast means the recent margin level does not directly support the idea of a steady earnings slide, even though the forecasts in the dataset still flag a material earnings risk.
Stay curious about how a 16.2% margin today lines up against models calling for multi year earnings declines. 🐻 Okasan Securities Group Bear Case

EPS trend builds across recent quarters

  • Basic EPS has moved from ¥10.62 in Q4 2025 to ¥8.57 in Q1 2026, ¥22.41 in Q2 2026 and ¥27.88 in Q3 2026, with trailing 12 month EPS at ¥69.41.
  • What is interesting for the prevailing bullish style of thinking around earnings resilience is that this step up in quarterly EPS and 69.41 yen of trailing 12 month EPS comes at the same time as forecasts in the dataset still point to future annual earnings decline of about 27.1%, so recent EPS strength alone does not erase those modelled risks.
    • Supporters of a more optimistic stance can point to Q3 2026 net income of ¥5,579 million versus ¥2,141 million in Q4 2025 and ¥2,833 million in Q3 2025, alongside the 7.9% trailing earnings growth figure.
    • At the same time, the projected multi year earnings decline means any bullish case has to explain why the run rate implied by ¥69.41 of trailing EPS might differ from the earnings path suggested in those forecasts.
If you are weighing that stepping EPS trend against the projected earnings decline, it is worth seeing how bullish investors join the dots. 🐂 Okasan Securities Group Bull Case

P/E discount meets DCF warning

  • At a share price of ¥884, the stock trades on a 12.7x P/E, below both the JP capital markets industry average of 15.9x and a peer average of 17.6x, while a DCF fair value of ¥457.62 in the dataset sits well under the current price.
  • Consensus style thinking that the stock could look inexpensive on simple multiples is challenged by that DCF fair value gap, because the lower 12.7x P/E and 7.9% trailing earnings growth are offset by the model that values future cash flows at roughly half the present market price.
    • The P/E discount might appeal to investors who focus on relative valuation, as the stock trades below both sector and peer multiples even with earnings at ¥13,920 million over the last 12 months.
    • However, the DCF fair value of ¥457.62 compared with ¥884, together with forecasts for multi year earnings and revenue decline, gives weight to a more cautious read of how much of that trailing performance is already reflected in the price.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Popular (BPOP) After Strong Multi Year Share Price Gains
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Popular's share price still reflects good value or if most of the opportunity is already priced in, this article walks through the key signals you can use to form your own view.
  • Popular's shares last closed at US$133.53, with returns of 9.5% over the past 7 days, 7.2% over 30 days, 6.0% year to date, 33.3% over 1 year, 106.6% over 3 years, and 149.1% over 5 years. These figures raise fair questions about what is now built into expectations and perceived risks.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Popular as a listed bank stock on the Nasdaq, with attention on how its share price performance compares to other financial institutions and what that might mean for investor sentiment. This context helps frame whether the current price level is driven more by company specific factors or by broader interest in bank stocks.
  • Popular currently has a valuation score of 5 out of 6, reflecting how many of our checks suggest the shares could be undervalued. Next, we will walk through the standard valuation approaches before finishing with a broader way to think about what that score really means.

Popular delivered 33.3% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Banks industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Popular Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company is expected to earn above the return that shareholders require, then capitalises those extra profits into an intrinsic value per share.

For Popular, the starting point is its balance sheet. Book value is $94.75 per share, with a stable book value estimate of $112.00 per share, based on weighted future estimates from 6 analysts. On that equity base, stable earnings are estimated at $15.24 per share, sourced from the same analyst set.

The model uses a cost of equity of $7.79 per share and an excess return of $7.45 per share. In other words, the earnings implied here sit above the required return, supported by an average return on equity of 13.60%. The Excess Returns framework converts those excess profits into an intrinsic value of about $313.47 per share.

Compared with the recent share price of US$133.53, this implies the shares trade at a 57.4% discount to this Excess Returns estimate. This indicates that the stock appears significantly undervalued on this basis.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests Popular is undervalued by 57.4%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 868 more undervalued stocks based on cash flows.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Popular.

Approach 2: Popular Price vs Earnings

For a profitable bank like Popular, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to relate what you pay per share to the earnings the business is generating today. It helps you see how many dollars of share price the market is attaching to each dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on what investors expect for future earnings and how risky they think those earnings are. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk often justify a higher P/E, while lower growth expectations or higher perceived risk usually line up with a lower P/E.

Popular currently trades on a P/E of 10.55x. That sits below the Banks industry average of 11.84x and below the peer group average of 12.99x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Popular is 13.01x, which represents the P/E that would be expected after considering factors such as earnings growth, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks. This Fair Ratio can be more informative than a simple comparison to peers or the industry, because it adjusts for those company specific characteristics. Since Popular’s Fair Ratio of 13.01x is higher than its current P/E of 10.55x, the shares appear undervalued on this measure.

Result: UNDERVALUED

data.title: Minerals Technologies (MTX) Trailing Loss Challenges Bullish Narrative After Q4 Profit
data.description:
Minerals Technologies (MTX) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of US$519.5 million and EPS of US$1.19, set against a 12‑month backdrop where trailing revenue sat at about US$2.1 billion and EPS was slightly negative at US$0.59. Over recent quarters the company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$518.1 million in Q4 2024 to US$528.9 million in Q2 2025 and US$532.4 million in Q3 2025 before landing at US$519.5 million in Q4 2025. Over the same period, EPS shifted from US$1.69 in Q4 2024 to a loss of US$4.51 in Q1 2025 and then back into the US$1.19 to US$1.44 range. This leaves investors focusing on how consistently the business can convert sales into profit. Margins are front and center here, with the latest numbers highlighting how sensitive overall profitability still is.

See our full analysis for Minerals Technologies.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up with the prevailing narratives around Minerals Technologies, and where the numbers start to challenge those stories.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

US$18.4 million trailing loss despite positive recent quarters

  • Over the last 12 months, Minerals Technologies reported a net loss of US$18.4 million even though the last three quarters of FY 2025 showed positive quarterly net income between US$37.1 million and US$45.4 million.
  • What stands out for a bearish view is that, despite those profitable quarters, trailing EPS is still a loss of US$0.59 and losses have grown at about 26.8% per year over five years. This sits awkwardly against the recent quarterly EPS range of roughly US$1.19 to US$1.44 and raises questions about how durable that improvement is.
    • Critics highlight that FY 2025 started with a quarterly loss of US$144 million, which is large relative to the later profits and is a big reason trailing earnings remain negative.
    • They also point out that trailing revenue of US$2.1b is only growing at about 4% per year, so weak earnings are not being offset by strong top line expansion.

Big swings between loss and profit often get value investors interested, but the 5 year earnings decline and US$18.4 million trailing loss keep the cautious camp vocal. 🐻 Minerals Technologies Bear Case

Revenue around US$2.1b, but growth running at 4%

  • On a trailing basis, revenue sits at about US$2.1b, with quarterly revenue over FY 2025 ranging between US$491.8 million and US$532.4 million, and the longer term revenue growth rate quoted at roughly 4% per year compared with a 10.6% US market reference.
  • Supporters taking a more optimistic stance argue that this steady US$2.1b revenue base across multiple quarters, even at 4% annual growth, underpins the business. However, the fact that earnings are still negative despite that scale and growth rate pulls against a simple bullish takeaway.
    • On one hand, quarterly revenue has hovered in a relatively tight band around US$520 million, which can appeal to investors who like consistent sales.
    • On the other hand, with that level of revenue, the trailing net loss of US$18.4 million shows that margins have not yet translated that stability into consistent profits.

DCF fair value at US$124.40 vs US$65.76 share price

  • The DCF fair value is given as US$124.40 per share compared with a current share price of US$65.76 and an analyst price target of US$83.00, so the stock is trading well below both those reference points while its P/S of roughly 1x is below the US chemicals industry at 1.2x and a 1.3x peer average.
  • This apparent discount heavily supports a bullish angle. At the same time, the same data set also shows an unprofitable trailing 12 months and multi year earnings declines of 26.8% per year, so investors weighing the DCF fair value and 83.00 price target against the current US$65.76 price have to balance low sales multiples and those valuation markers against the fact that net income is still a US$18.4 million loss.
    • Analysts pointing to potential upside can lean on both the DCF fair value and the price target being above the current share price while P/S sits below industry and peers.
    • More cautious investors may focus on the negative trailing EPS of US$0.59 and the weak earnings trend as a reason the discount could persist until profitability improves.

The gap between DCF fair value, analyst targets and today’s price is large enough that many investors will want to see how future reports address the US$18.4 million trailing loss before leaning too hard on the apparent discount. 📊 Read the full Minerals Technologies Consensus Narrative.

data.title: Comcast (CMCSA) Margin Strength Reinforces Bullish Narratives Despite Flat Revenue Trend
data.description:
Comcast (CMCSA) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of US$32.3b and basic EPS of US$0.60, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of US$123.7b and EPS of US$5.39. Over the last six reported quarters, revenue has ranged from US$29.9b to US$32.3b, while quarterly EPS has moved between US$0.60 and US$2.99. This gives investors a clear view of how earnings per share translate off a large and relatively steady top line base. With a trailing net margin of 16.2% against 13.1% a year earlier and five year earnings growth in the double digits, the latest numbers frame Comcast as a scale player where the quality of margins sits at the center of the earnings story.

See our full analysis for Comcast.

With the results on the table, the next step is to set these figures against the widely held narratives around Comcast to see which storylines the numbers support and which ones they start to challenge.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Margins and profit growth outpace modest 0.2% revenue trend

  • Over the last five years, earnings have grown 13.7% per year, with a 23.5% gain in the most recent year, while forecast revenue growth in the data sits at 0.2% per year. This suggests profitability is doing more of the heavy lifting than the top line.
  • What is interesting for the bullish view is that this mix of slow revenue growth and stronger profit growth lines up with a company leaning on a recurring, utility like connectivity base,
    • trailing net margin is 16.2% compared with 13.1% a year earlier, which fits the idea that an established broadband and media group can keep squeezing value out of its existing scale,
    • yet the same bullish argument has to square those profit trends with the very low 0.2% revenue growth figure, which suggests most of the recent strength has come from margins rather than expanding sales.

13.2% forecast EPS decline clashes with 23.5% past growth

  • The data shows trailing earnings growing 13.7% per year over five years and 23.5% over the last year, while analysts in the dataset expect earnings to fall by about 13.2% per year over the next three years.
  • Bears argue that the projected 13.2% annual EPS decline is a key risk,
    • and that view is backed by the explicit forecast for multi year earnings contraction, which sits in sharp contrast to the recent 23.5% one year earnings rise,
    • so anyone leaning bearish can point to that swing from strong historical growth to expected declines as a sign that the last few years of improvement may not repeat in the near term.
Stay alert to how quickly the story could shift if those forecast EPS declines materialize against a backdrop of strong trailing growth and rising margins. 🐻 Comcast Bear Case

P/E of 5.4x and US$77.93 DCF fair value versus US$29.75 price

  • Comcast trades on a trailing P/E of 5.4x compared with a 6.2x peer average and 16.4x for the Global Telecom industry, and the supplied DCF fair value is US$77.93 per share versus a current share price of US$29.75.
  • Supporters of a bullish angle point to this combination of low multiple and high DCF fair value,
    • since the P/E discount to both peers and the wider telecom group lines up with the idea that the market is not fully crediting the 16.2% net margin and 13.7% five year annual earnings growth,
    • and the gap between the US$29.75 price and the US$77.93 DCF fair value in the data gives bulls a clear numerical anchor if they think the projected 13.2% yearly earnings decline is already more than reflected in today’s valuation.

data.title: Leidos Targets 2030 With ENTRUST Deal And OpenAI AI Push
data.description:
• Leidos Holdings (NYSE:LDOS) announced the acquisition of ENTRUST Solutions Group, expanding its reach in energy infrastructure services.
  • The company also entered a partnership with OpenAI to integrate generative and agentic AI tools into core government, health, defense, and infrastructure workflows.
  • Both moves are positioned as key steps in advancing Leidos’ NorthStar 2030 strategy and reshaping its business mix.

Leidos, a major contractor across defense, intelligence, civil, and health markets, is using the ENTRUST acquisition to add more exposure to energy infrastructure work. That places NYSE:LDOS in the middle of ongoing grid modernization, utility reliability, and critical infrastructure resilience efforts that many governments and utilities are prioritizing.

The OpenAI partnership points to a heavier use of AI in day to day missions for government and infrastructure clients, from workflow automation to decision support. For investors, these moves are essentially about where Leidos wants its revenue mix and capabilities to be by 2030, and how AI and energy infrastructure could influence that path.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Leidos Holdings by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Leidos Holdings.

Why Leidos Holdings could be great value

For investors, the ENTRUST deal and OpenAI alliance point to Leidos pushing deeper into two areas that many of its government and utility customers care about right now: grid reliability and AI-powered productivity. The roughly US$2.4b all-cash acquisition, partly funded with a US$1.4b bridge facility, increases Leidos’ exposure to regulated utility work. The OpenAI partnership is aimed at embedding generative and agentic AI in everyday workflows across defense, civil and health programs, where peers like Booz Allen Hamilton and Science Applications International are also active.

Advertisement

How this fits the Leidos Holdings narrative

The news lines up with the existing narrative that Leidos is leaning into AI integration and acquisitions to improve efficiency and support higher quality contracts. Management is also using deals like ENTRUST to shift the business mix toward more recurring, service-based work, which the narrative frames as helpful for earnings visibility and contract win rates across core federal and infrastructure customers.

Key risks and rewards investors are weighing

  • The ENTRUST acquisition increases Leidos’ presence in utility and infrastructure engineering, which could deepen relationships with large, long-cycle customers.
  • Wider AI use through OpenAI’s tools may support faster product delivery and lower indirect costs for both internal teams and client projects.
  • Funding the US$2.4b deal with new debt and liquidity adds to balance sheet commitments, and analysts have already flagged that Leidos carries a high level of debt.
  • Acquisition integration and AI execution risk remain, especially as peers like RTX and Booz Allen compete aggressively for similar government and infrastructure budgets.

What to watch next

From here, you may want to track regulatory approvals and closing timing for the ENTRUST deal, how quickly Leidos wins new utility or grid-modernization work, and any commentary on AI adoption from upcoming earnings calls and investor updates.
